title: Listening - Part 4: Mapping Underground Caves
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Grace, a volunteer cave mapper. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m talking to Grace Liu, who explores and maps underground caves. Grace, where did that interest begin? || Grace: I was not particularly adventurous at school. During a family holiday, we visited a public cave with lights and proper paths. The guide explained that passages continued beyond the visitor route. [24]The thought that unknown spaces could exist under ordinary fields made me eager to discover more[/24]. I joined a caving club when I was old enough. || Presenter: What did the club teach you first? || Grace: I expected to begin by squeezing through exciting tunnels, but responsible clubs do not work like that. We learned equipment checks, communication and weather risks. [25]Before exploring anywhere difficult, I had to prove I could move carefully and help another person in a safe training cave[/25]. That discipline is why the activity remains enjoyable rather than foolish. || Presenter: Why is mapping important? || Grace: A map helps explorers find routes, of course, but it does more than that. Underground water can affect towns and farms above. [26]Accurate cave maps allow scientists to understand where water travels and which areas may be vulnerable to pollution[/26]. Some of our weekend measurements later become useful to environmental researchers. || Presenter: Does modern equipment make mapping simple? || Grace: Laser devices calculate distances faster than older tape measures, but underground conditions remain awkward. Mud covers equipment, and narrow spaces do not give you much room. [27]The hardest part is maintaining careful records when you are cold, wet and keen to continue exploring[/27]. A small error below ground may create a confusing map back at home. || Presenter: Have you ever been frightened? || Grace: I have been uncomfortable, especially once when rain began earlier than predicted during a trip. We had chosen a route that remained safe, but the sound of water increased. [28]That experience taught me never to treat a weather forecast as a guarantee when planning an underground journey[/28]. Now I always check escape options before going in. || Presenter: Some caves are fragile environments. How do you protect them? || Grace: Formations that took thousands of years to grow can break with one careless hand. There can also be rare creatures. [29]Our group shares locations cautiously and marks routes that keep visitors away from delicate formations[/29]. Discovering a place does not give us the right to damage it or invite unlimited traffic. || Presenter: What keeps you doing this in your free time? || Grace: It is not about being the first person somewhere; that hardly ever happens. [30]I enjoy turning a dark, confusing network of passages into information that other people can safely use and learn from[/30]. It combines physical exploration, careful teamwork and a practical result, which is exactly what I like.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first made Grace curious about caves?

options.A: Finding an entrance close to her home.
options.B: Hearing about unexplored passages on a visit.

questions.explanation: A guide explained that passages extended beyond the public cave route.

questions.question: What did Grace have to demonstrate before difficult exploration?

options.A: That she could draw a map quickly.
options.C: That she could act safely and assist someone else.

questions.explanation: Training required careful movement and helping another person in a safe cave.

questions.question: Why are cave maps useful to scientists?

options.A: They can show possible movement of water.
options.B: They record the age of rock formations.
options.C: They help identify new animal species.

questions.explanation: Maps help scientists understand water paths and pollution vulnerability.

questions.question: What does Grace find most challenging during mapping?

options.A: Using laser equipment in small passages.
options.B: Recording information accurately in unpleasant conditions.
options.C: Remembering routes without a completed map.

questions.explanation: It is difficult to maintain careful records when wet and cold.

questions.question: What did an experience with rain teach Grace?

options.A: Forecasts cannot be completely relied on.
options.B: Caves are safer during heavy rainfall.

questions.explanation: She no longer treats a weather forecast as a guarantee.

questions.question: How does Grace's group protect caves?

options.A: It repairs formations damaged by visitors.
options.B: It stops all members entering new caves.
options.C: It limits location details and follows protective routes.

questions.explanation: They share locations carefully and mark paths away from fragile formations.

questions.question: What does Grace most appreciate about caving?

options.A: Being first to explore new locations.

questions.explanation: She enjoys converting confusing passages into information others can safely use.
